questions.stem: Members of a hospital ethics committee individually lean slightly toward approving an experimental treatment protocol. After group discussion, the committee votes to approve the protocol with significantly more enthusiasm and fewer restrictions than any individual member originally favored. This shift is best explained by:
questions.explanation: Group polarization describes the tendency for groups to make more extreme decisions than individual members would make alone. After discussion, the group shifts toward a more intense version of the average initial position. Since committee members initially leaned toward approval, group discussion amplified that inclination into stronger approval with fewer restrictions. Groupthink involves suppressing dissent to maintain consensus, but there is no indication of dissent suppression here. Social loafing involves reduced individual effort in groups. Deindividuation involves loss of self-awareness and behavioral restraints in crowds.
questions.kn_explanation: This question addresses group decision-making processes, specifically group polarization, a core social psychology concept.
questions.quality_comment: Tests application of group polarization in a realistic clinical scenario, requiring differentiation from groupthink.
questions.suggestedParagraph: ## Group Polarization: When Groups Amplify Extremes
questions.lessonExcerpt: Group polarization describes the tendency for groups to make more extreme decisions than individual members would make alone. After discussion, the group shifts toward a more intense version of the average initial position. Either riskier or more cautious.
questions.inspirationDomain: SOCIAL AND MULTICULTURAL BASES OF BEHAVIOR

questions.stem: Research on group composition in decision-making contexts has found that heterogeneous groups, compared to homogeneous groups, tend to:
options: Make better decisions but may have more difficulty with cohesion
Make worse decisions but have stronger cohesion
Make equivalent decisions with better cohesion
Make better decisions with equivalent cohesion
questions.answer: Make better decisions but may have more difficulty with cohesion
questions.explanation: Research on group effectiveness indicates that heterogeneous groups make better decisions than homogeneous groups because diverse perspectives lead to more comprehensive analysis. However, heterogeneous groups can experience problems with cohesion due to differences among members. This represents a trade-off between decision quality and group harmony. The other options incorrectly characterize either the decision quality or cohesion outcomes of heterogeneous versus homogeneous groups.
questions.kn_explanation: This question addresses group composition and performance, a social psychology topic relevant to both clinical group work and organizational settings.
questions.quality_comment: Directly supported by reference text content on group effectiveness.
questions.lessonExcerpt: Heterogeneous groups make better decisions than homogeneous groups. However, there can be problems with cohesion in heterogeneous groups.
questions.inspirationDomain: SOCIAL AND MULTICULTURAL BASES OF BEHAVIOR

questions.stem: A multidisciplinary treatment team at a prestigious hospital is discussing discharge planning for a high-risk patient. The team is highly cohesive and led by an attending physician who states her preference for early discharge before inviting input. Team members privately have concerns but remain silent, interpreting others' silence as agreement. One senior nurse redirects conversations away from a junior staff member's attempt to raise safety data. Which combination of groupthink symptoms is MOST evident in this scenario?
options: Illusion of invulnerability, collective rationalization, and excessive stereotyping
Self-censorship, illusion of unanimity, and mindguards
Belief in inherent morality, collective rationalization, and self-censorship
Excessive stereotyping, illusion of invulnerability, and mindguards
questions.answer: Self-censorship, illusion of unanimity, and mindguards
questions.explanation: The scenario illustrates three specific groupthink symptoms identified by Janis: (1) Self-censorship—members suppress their private doubts to maintain harmony; (2) Illusion of unanimity—silence is interpreted as agreement, when in fact members have unexpressed concerns; (3) Mindguards—the senior nurse acts as a self-appointed protector who shields the group from contradictory information by redirecting away from the junior staff member's safety data. Option A is incorrect because the scenario does not describe the group feeling invulnerable to failure or dismissing warnings as irrelevant, nor stereotyping critics. Option C is incorrect because there is no mention of the group believing their decisions are inherently ethical (belief in inherent morality) or dismissing external warnings (collective rationalization). Option D is incorrect because there is no evidence of the group viewing critics as incompetent or feeling invulnerable.
questions.kn_explanation: KN12 covers group dynamics and decision-making processes including groupthink as a threat to quality group decisions, a core topic in social psychology.
questions.quality_comment: Complex scenario requiring examinees to match specific observable behaviors to Janis's groupthink symptoms rather than simply recognizing the concept. Tests discrimination among symptom clusters.
questions.lessonExcerpt: Self-censorship: Members suppress doubts to maintain harmony. Illusion of unanimity: Silence is interpreted as agreement. Mindguards: Self-appointed members shield the group from contradictory information.
questions.inspirationDomain: SOCIAL AND MULTICULTURAL BASES OF BEHAVIOR
